narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 50.3% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your third-decile ranking in Year 1 grades, second-decile in Year 3, LSAT score of 35, undergraduate GPA of 3.4, middle family income bracket, female gender, White racial background, and full-time study plans. Compared to typical students, your early and later school deciles are notably lower than the averages around the fifth or sixth decile, your LSAT is a bit below the usual 36.7, but your GPA stands out positively above the 3.21 average. The model's decision was most heavily swayed by your Year 1 decile ranking, which pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure more than any other factor. Next in influence was your LSAT score, also nudging the odds toward failure, followed closely by your middle family income. Your Year 3 decile came after that, contributing similarly in the failure direction, with your racial background having a moderate pull in the same way. Your undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status had progressively smaller influences, all still leaning slightly toward the failure prediction but much less decisively. Overall, these factors combined to tip the balance just over 50% failure risk, unlike profiles with stronger early academics that often predict success. While demographics like gender and race aren't changeable, you can realistically boost your chances by retaking the LSAT to aim higher than 35, as even a small improvement there could shift the prediction favorably given its high influence. Focusing on building a stronger academic record, perhaps through additional coursework or targeted prep, might help offset those lower deciles in future evaluations. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by prioritizing bar-specific study habits early on. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these areas—you have a solid GPA foundation to build from. Keep pushing forward; your determination will make a difference.
